title: Lavender bath for restlessness and mild insomnia
subtitle: A relaxing herb bath to unwind before sleep
excerpt: A strong lavender infusion added to the evening bath. The volatile oils absorb through the skin and are inhaled with the steam, promoting relaxation before sleep. One of the oldest traditional applications of lavender in European domestic medicine.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), lavender entry, nervine and sedative properties, aromatic use for anxiety and sleeplessness. EMA HMPC monograph on Lavandulae flos (2011), confirms traditional use of lavender for mild anxiety, restlessness, and sleep disturbance via inhalation and topical routes.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Prepare the infusion while the bath is filling. Dried lavender keeps 12 months in a sealed tin.

recipeTools.notes: For steeping the concentrated lavender infusion.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb traditionally used to calm and support the nervous system, easing tension, restlessness, and mild anxiety.

content.text: A strong lavender infusion poured into the evening bath. Lavender is the tradition's most reliable

for restlessness and mild insomnia, its linalool and linalyl acetate volatile oil constituents act on the nervous system through both inhalation and skin absorption. The EMA's 2011 monograph confirms traditional use of lavender for mild anxiety, restlessness, and sleep disturbance. Making the bath is straightforward: brew a concentrated infusion from dried lavender flowers, strain it, and add it to a warm, not hot, bath taken 30 to 60 minutes before bed.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Skin: dried lavender bath preparations are generally well tolerated. Test on a small area of skin if you have sensitive skin or eczema before a full bath.

Bath temperature: for a sedative effect, keep the bath warm rather than hot (around 37, 38°C). Very hot baths are stimulating rather than relaxing and can cause faintness on standing.

Pregnancy: lavender aromatherapy at normal bath concentrations is widely used in pregnancy and is not associated with adverse effects. Very high-dose concentrated lavender preparations are, as with all herbs, best discussed with a midwife during the first trimester.

See your doctor for insomnia lasting more than four weeks, insomnia accompanied by mood changes, or if you suspect your sleep disruption has an underlying cause such as sleep apnoea.

items.prepNote: a generous handful, this makes a concentrated infusion that goes into a full bath
just off the boil, for the concentrate, the bath will dilute this greatly

content.text: Place {{dried-lavender-flowers}} of dried lavender flowers in the saucepan. Pour {{water}} of just-off-boil water over. Lid the pan and steep for 10 minutes. The liquid will be dark purple-grey and smell strongly of lavender. This is the concentrate that will go into the bath.
Strain and add to the bath

Strain the infusion through a sieve directly into the bath as it fills. Discard the spent flowers. A warm bath (37, 38°C) dilutes the concentrate to a comfortable bathing concentration while remaining aromatic. Soak for 15 to 20 minutes, 30 to 60 minutes before bed.

One lavender bath in the evening, as part of a wind-down routine before bed. Can be used nightly during a period of mild restlessness, there are no safety concerns with regular lavender baths at this strength. For persistent insomnia lasting more than four weeks, the bath is a supportive measure only, see your doctor.
When NOT to use this remedy

See your doctor for insomnia that has lasted more than four weeks, insomnia with mood changes, excessive daytime sleepiness, or snoring and waking episodes that suggest sleep apnoea. The lavender bath is a preparation for mild, situational restlessness, not a treatment for a sleep disorder.
